params.text:
The hidden risk: emollient creams

Emollients are moisturising creams, ointments and lotions used every day in care to treat dry or fragile skin. Many people do not realise they carry a serious fire risk. When emollient rubs off the skin onto clothing, bedding, dressings or upholstery, it soaks into the fabric and dries there. This residue is highly flammable – if it comes near a cigarette, lighter or naked flame it can ignite quickly and burn fiercely, causing severe or fatal burns.

This applies to all emollients, whether or not they contain paraffin, and it builds up gradually and invisibly over time. There have been many fire deaths in the UK linked to emollient-soaked fabric, and the risk is well recognised by the CQC, the MHRA and fire services.

Reducing the risk

  • Keep anyone using emollients well away from cigarettes, lighters, matches, cookers, heaters and any naked flame.
  • Wash clothing, bedding and towels that contact emollient regularly – daily where possible – to reduce residue build-up. Note that normal washing does not fully remove it.
  • Change dressings used with emollients daily.
  • Never smoke in bed or in a chair where emollient residue may have built up, and record the risk in the person's care plan.

params.question: Only emollient creams that contain paraffin are a fire risk; paraffin-free creams are safe near naked flames.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Incorrect. All emollients – paraffin-based or not – raise the fire risk once they soak into clothing or bedding. Everyone using them should be kept clear of flames.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. ALL emollients increase flammability when soaked into fabric, whether or not they contain paraffin. Anyone using them must be kept away from flames and heat.
